planting.note: Plant container stock or scarified, soaked seeds in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; Guanacaste is the national tree of Costa Rica and one of the most impressive shade trees of Central America — it is fast-growing and drought-tolerant once established but requires protection from frost below 30°F.

directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper and soak overnight before sowing; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, deep soil in full sun; provide frost protection when young. Enterolobium cyclocarpum (Guanacaste or elephant ear tree) is one of the most massive and magnificent tropical shade trees of Central America — the national tree of Costa Rica — growing to an enormous size with a broadly spreading crown reaching 80–100 feet in diameter that provides extraordinary shade. The common name 'elephant ear tree' comes from the distinctive, flat, dark-brown, ear-shaped seed pods 2–4 inches across that resemble a stylized elephant ear. The tree produces fluffy, white, powder-puff flowers followed by distinctive coiled pods containing 6–15 seeds surrounded by edible, sweet, pulpy flesh used in traditional beverages in Costa Rica. The seeds are high in protein and have been used as animal feed. The wood is valuable for furniture and construction throughout Central America. In zone 10–12 gardens with no frost risk, the tree is a spectacular choice for large-scale tropical landscaping where its enormous size can be accommodated; it grows fast (to 15–20 feet in 3–4 years) and eventually dominates the landscape.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Trees

planting.note: Direct sow or allow to self-seed in any well-drained, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; hairy beggartick is a Pantropical annual that volunteers in disturbed soil and garden margins throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain — it is a widely used edible and medicinal herb in African, Asian, and tropical American cuisines and folk medicine.

directions: Sow seeds or allow to self-seed in any well-drained, moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; harvest young leaves and shoots for culinary use before flowering. Bidens pilosa (hairy beggartick or black jack) is one of the world's most widespread tropical annual herbs — a common 'weed' of disturbed soils throughout the tropical and subtropical world that is simultaneously one of the most widely used edible and medicinal plants in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. In many African countries (particularly Kenya, Zimbabwe, and Central Africa), the young shoots and leaves are a major leafy vegetable cooked in stews and soups; in Asia, it is cooked like spinach; in Brazil and the Caribbean, it is used as a medicinal tea. The plant is a prolific producer of small, black seed-needles with two barbed prongs (the 'beggar ticks' or 'Spanish needles' that attach to clothing and animal fur). Despite its weedy character in gardens, the small, white, daisy-like flowers with yellow centers are important nectar sources for native bees and butterflies. Young leaves are mildly flavored and nutritious, used in many cultures as a green vegetable. A genuinely dual-use plant — volunteer edible and medicinal herb.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Herbs
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Tropical Edible Weeds

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or slips in spring after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; ornamental sweet potato vines are among the most vigorous and colorful summer trailing annuals for beds, borders, and containers in zone 9 and perform outstandingly through Gulf Coast heat.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in warm soil after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; ornamental sweet potato grows extremely vigorously and may need periodic trimming to keep in bounds. Ipomoea batatas 'Tricolor' (ornamental sweet potato vine) is one of the most widely grown summer ornamental foliage annuals — a vigorous, trailing or mounding, tender perennial vine grown for its spectacular, large (3–5 inch), deeply lobed or heart-shaped leaves in bold foliage colors ranging from chartreuse to lime green ('Margarita'), deep burgundy-purple to nearly black ('Blackie'), and multicolored ('Tricolor' with pink, white, and green variegation). The plants trail vigorously up to 3–8 feet during the summer season and are used as groundcovers, in hanging baskets, and as 'spillers' in container combinations. Unlike edible sweet potatoes, the ornamental varieties have been selected for foliage rather than tuber quality, though the small tubers produced are edible. Ornamental sweet potato vine performs outstandingly in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and is one of the few colorful trailing plants that maintains vigor through August heat. Note: the leaves and small tubers are mildly toxic to cats and dogs.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Summer Annuals
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Container Plants

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or bedding transplants in spring after frost danger has passed in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver falls dichondra is one of the most effective and dramatic trailing annuals for hanging baskets and container 'spillers,' producing cascading curtains of silver foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver falls dichondra is drought-tolerant and more vigorous in warm, dry conditions than in humid, wet ones. Dichondra argentea (silver ponysfoot or Silver Falls dichondra) is a Texas and Mexican native trailing plant producing cascading stems of small (3/4-inch), perfectly round, silver-white, densely hairy leaves that create a spectacular waterfall of shimmering, reflective foliage when allowed to trail from hanging baskets, window boxes, or retaining walls — the 'Silver Falls' selection is grown primarily for this effect. The silver color comes from dense white hairs covering the round, kidney-shaped to circular leaves. Unlike many trailing annuals, Silver Falls is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant, often performing better in dry conditions than in humid, rainy weather (which can cause stem rot). It is one of the most effective annual 'spillers' in container gardening for creating dramatic, flowing, silver curtain effects. The plant's native habitat is the dry rocky slopes and cliffs of the Chihuahuan Desert and Trans-Pecos Texas. In zone 9, it may survive mild winters as a semi-perennial; it dies back in cold but resprouts if roots are protected.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Container Plants
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Vines
